title: Pfizer — Relationships
html:
The Pfizer-ZoomRx Relationship

Governing Thought

ZoomRx's Pfizer relationship is young (~2-3 years), vaccines-concentrated, and at an inflection point. The partnership has established deep credibility in HCP-Patient conversations — now running on four separate vaccine brands — and is recognized as an AI pioneer through the Abrysvo PET AI work. But the relationship lacks the cross-TA breadth, enterprise-level entrenchment, and annualized contract structure that define ZoomRx's J&J partnership. The path from $302K to $575K (and beyond) depends on converting brand-level HCPP trust into multi-service relationships and entering non-vaccine territories. The single biggest structural barrier is Pfizer's independent brand organization — there is no franchise-level buyer, so every expansion is a new sale.

Partnership Timeline

Year Milestone
~2023-2024 Relationship established through Abrysvo Maternal work; early HCPP and PET engagements begin
2024 Abrysvo OA HCP-Patient conversations study (n=10); RSV PET tracking waves active
2024 Abrysvo Adult PET lost (budget cut / re-org — noted as a SWOT threat precedent)
2024-2025 HCPP expansion: Trumenba/Penbraya HCPP launched; Prevnar Adult HCPP signed
2025 Pneumococcal HCP-Patient conversations study (n=20) — most insight-rich study to date
2025 Lisa Dixon departs — loss of primary champion on Abrysvo Maternal
2025-2026 Abrysvo OA PET signed; Abrysvo Maternal Digital Tracking in discussion
2026 Meningococcal ATU study launched
2026 Message Labs AI Migraine Messaging Pilot with Aimee Renaud — first non-vaccine project
2026 Lorbrena PET — first oncology project
2026 Pharmacist Quant study — cross-vaccine pharmacy channel research

Key statistic: The partnership spans 1 TA (Vaccines) with 4 brands actively engaged and 2 emerging non-vaccine projects (Message Labs, Lorbrena PET). 10+ project engagements completed or active in the last 12 months.

Why Pfizer Chose ZoomRx (and Keeps Choosing)

Pillar 1: HCP-Patient Conversation Methodology

ZoomRx's HCPP is the service that opened the Pfizer door and keeps it open. Unlike Verilogue's transcript-and-coding approach, ZoomRx delivers AI-extracted themes, behavioral patterns, and specific messaging recommendations. The methodology uniquely captures what happens in the exam room — a data source no other research vendor provides at ZoomRx's level of analytical depth.

Evidence:

  • 4 separate vaccine brands now use HCPP (Abrysvo Maternal, Abrysvo OA, Prevnar Adult, Trumenba/Penbraya)
  • Cross-study findings (communication style driving acceptance, brand-agnostic recommendation patterns) demonstrate portfolio-level value that transcends individual brand studies
  • HCPP is becoming "entrenched" — Pfizer teams treat it as a standard part of their insights toolkit, not a one-off experiment

Pillar 2: AI Capabilities and Reputation

ZoomRx is recognized at Pfizer as an AI pioneer. The Abrysvo PET AI enhancements established this reputation, and the Message Labs AI Migraine Messaging Pilot with Aimee Renaud (AI Excellence) extends it beyond vaccines. In a company where marketing leads hold significant vendor-selection sway, "AI pioneer" status is a door-opener that transcends brand boundaries.

Evidence:

  • AO plan SWOT explicitly lists "Making a name as AI pioneers/experts, notably through AI capabilities on Abrysvo PETs" as a core strength
  • Aimee Renaud (Pfizer AI Excellence) engaged directly with ZoomRx for the Message Labs pilot — above-brand recognition of AI capabilities
  • AI reputation is the primary path to non-vaccine entry; Pfizer's brand teams are independently evaluating AI-enhanced insights vendors

Pillar 3: Actionable Output Differentiation

ZoomRx deliverables at Pfizer go beyond data presentation to provide specific, brand-relevant recommendations. The Pneumococcal HCP-Pt study, for example, didn't just show that assertive communication drives acceptance — it quantified the effect size (100% vs. 60%), mapped it to patient segments, and identified specific behavioral levers (in-office stocking, presumptive language, disease severity communication) that marketing can act on.

Evidence:

  • Prevnar 20 "one and done" messaging recommendation directly informed brand strategy
  • Serotype counting competitive intelligence flagged Capvaxive threat before Pfizer's own competitive intelligence identified the magnitude
  • Communication style finding (assertive vs. suggestive) is a portfolio-level behavioral insight that no competitor research firm has produced

Proven Impact: Key Deliverables

1. Pneumococcal HCP-Patient Conversations (Prevnar 20)

Challenge: Pfizer needed to understand what actually happens when HCPs recommend pneumococcal vaccination — what drives patient acceptance, what causes deferral, and how competitors (Capvaxive) are being positioned in real conversations. Approach: ZoomRx recorded and AI-analyzed 20 real HCP-Patient conversations across primary care, pulmonology, and pharmacy settings. Impact: Identified the communication style effect (assertive = 100% acceptance vs. suggestive = 60%), the serotype counting competitive risk (Capvaxive winning 3/7 head-to-head discussions), the pharmacy referral drop-off risk, and the disease severity under-communication pattern. These findings shaped Pfizer's Prevnar 20 messaging strategy.

2. Abrysvo OA HCP-Patient Conversations (RSV Adults)

Challenge: Pfizer needed to understand how HCPs discuss RSV vaccination with older adults in a market with 3 competing products and narrowed CDC recommendations. Approach: ZoomRx analyzed 10 real HCP-Patient conversations focusing on recommendation patterns, brand mentions, and severity communication. Impact: Revealed that 8/10 HCPs made brand-agnostic RSV vaccine recommendations (not naming Abrysvo), and 7/10 under-communicated RSV disease severity. This finding reframed Pfizer's challenge from "win brand preference" to "ensure your brand gets named when physicians recommend."

3. Abrysvo Maternal PET (RSV Promotional Effectiveness)

Challenge: Track rep effectiveness in promoting Abrysvo for maternal RSV vaccination in a seasonally compressed market with nirsevimab competition. Approach: Quantitative PET tracking multiple waves measuring message recall, physician awareness, and prescribing intent. Impact: Provided wave-over-wave tracking that informed field force messaging adjustments. AI-enhanced analytics on this PET established ZoomRx's AI reputation at Pfizer.

4. Message Labs — AI Migraine Messaging Pilot

Challenge: Pfizer's AI Excellence team (Aimee Renaud) wanted to pilot AI-powered message testing methodology in migraine (Nurtec context). Approach: ZoomRx's Message Labs platform used AI to test and optimize brand messages with real HCPs. Impact: Represents the first non-vaccine ZoomRx project at Pfizer. Establishes the AI capabilities proof point for above-brand teams and creates a precedent for expanding into primary care and specialty brands.

5. Respiratory Vaccine Pharmacist Pain Points Pulse Survey

Challenge: Pfizer needed to understand pharmacist perspectives across the vaccine portfolio — what friction points exist in the pharmacy channel for RSV, pneumococcal, and meningococcal vaccination. Approach: Pulse survey targeting pharmacists on pain points, stocking decisions, and patient referral dynamics. Impact: Addressed the pharmacy channel gap identified in HCPP studies (pharmacy referral as a drop-off risk). Cross-portfolio design demonstrated ZoomRx's ability to deliver franchise-level insights even within Pfizer's brand-independent structure.

Growth Trajectory & BD Pipeline

The relationship is expanding along three vectors:

Vector 1: Service Expansion Within Vaccines

The HCPP-to-PET-to-ATU upsell path is the core revenue driver:

  • HCPP is established on 4 brands (Abrysvo Maternal, Abrysvo OA, Prevnar Adult, Trumenba/Penbraya)
  • PET is active on 2 brands (Abrysvo Maternal, Abrysvo OA) with pilots planned for Trumenba and Prevnar Adult
  • ATU is the next service to add — Abrysvo Maternal ATU would displace Sermo
  • Digital Tracking is in discussion for Trumenba/Penbraya and active on Abrysvo Maternal

Each service added on an existing brand deepens the relationship and increases switching costs.

Vector 2: Non-Vaccine Brand Entry

The FY26 plan explicitly targets $125K from "other brands." The entry points are:

  • Paxlovid (Gavin Hansen, warm referral) — HCPP
  • Nurtec (via Tim Douglas or Aimee Renaud intro) — HCPP or Message Labs
  • Xeljanz / Vyndaquel (via Aimee Renaud or Josh Stewart) — HCPP
  • Lorbrena (PET already active) — oncology foothold

The Message Labs AI Migraine Pilot with Aimee Renaud is the most strategically important project on the account right now — it proves ZoomRx capabilities beyond vaccines and creates above-brand advocacy.

Vector 3: BI PET Displacement

BI runs most Pfizer PETs. Displacing even 1-2 PETs would add $75-150K in annual revenue and shift Pfizer's perception of ZoomRx from "the HCPP vendor" to "a full-service tracking partner." The AO plan calls for zero-cost PET pilots in Q2 FY26 on Trumenba or Prevnar Adult.

Rules of Engagement

Based on AO plan insights and observed Pfizer dynamics:

  1. Each brand is a separate sale. There is no franchise buyer. Marketing leads control vendor decisions independently. Every pitch must be tailored to the specific brand's problems.

  2. Marketing leads hold significant sway. Unlike J&J where Insights teams (SFEA, OCE leads) drive vendor selection, at Pfizer the marketing leads are deeply involved. Frame research in marketing terms (messaging optimization, brand positioning, competitive displacement), not just data collection terms.

  3. AI reputation is a universal door-opener. Pfizer values AI capabilities across the organization (Aimee Renaud's role exists for this reason). Every proposal should lead with AI-enhanced methodology and ZoomRx's track record as an AI pioneer at Pfizer.

  4. HCPP is the wedge product — but the goal is multi-service. HCPP gets ZoomRx in the door ($50-75K, novel, fast). But account growth depends on upselling to PET ($75-150K, recurring), ATU ($50-75K, competitive displacement), and beyond. A brand with only HCPP is undermonetized.

  5. Proof points must be brand-specific. Showing Abrysvo results to the Nurtec team is helpful for credibility, but the Nurtec team will judge ZoomRx on whether HCPP solves their problem. Tailor every case study to the target brand's specific competitive dynamics and patient population.

  6. Referrals are the growth engine. Tim Douglas's satisfaction with Abrysvo HCPP led to Prevnar Adult HCPP. Explicitly ask established contacts: "Is there a colleague on another brand who might benefit from this?" This replaces the franchise-selling model that Pfizer's org structure makes impossible.

Competitive Threats to the Relationship

Threat 1: BI — Deeply Entrenched PET Incumbent

BI runs most Pfizer PETs with long-running tracker relationships and established workflows. The threat is not that BI will take HCPP business (they don't compete there) but that BI's PET entrenchment prevents ZoomRx from expanding into the highest-revenue service category.

Mitigation: Flank, don't attack. The HCPP-to-PET upsell path leverages ZoomRx's unique qualitative insights as a differentiation layer that BI cannot match. Zero-cost pilots on brands where ZoomRx already runs HCPP (Trumenba, Prevnar Adult) minimize risk for the client. AI-enhanced PET analytics (faster turnaround, automated insight extraction) position ZoomRx as the next-generation alternative.

Threat 2: Verilogue — HCP-Patient Incumbent on Select Brands

Verilogue runs HCP-Patient conversations at Pfizer, including Prevnar Peds (Stephanie Archer's team). They are a direct competitor to ZoomRx's core wedge product.

Mitigation: Differentiate on output, not methodology. Verilogue provides transcripts with basic coding; ZoomRx provides AI-extracted themes and specific messaging recommendations. On brands where both are present, the deliverable quality difference should be visible. On new brands where neither is present, move faster. Coexistence is acceptable in the near term.

Threat 3: Sermo — ATU Incumbent

Sermo runs ATU on Abrysvo Maternal and likely other vaccine brands. Their standalone ATU sits in isolation — no connection to HCPP or PET insights on the same brand.

Mitigation: The integrated insights story. On brands where ZoomRx runs HCPP + PET, adding ATU creates a three-lens unified view (exam room conversations, field effectiveness, market awareness) that Sermo cannot match. Bundled pricing within a brand makes consolidation economically rational for the client.

Threat 4: Budget Cuts and Re-Orgs

The SWOT explicitly notes "re-orgs or budget cuts (similar to Abrysvo Adult PET loss last year)" as a threat, though assessed as low risk currently.

Mitigation: Brand diversification reduces the impact of any single brand's budget cut. Building relationships with multiple decision makers across brands ensures that even if one brand's budget is cut, the account relationship survives through other brands.

Threat 5: Lisa Dixon Departure — Champion Gap

The loss of Lisa Dixon removed ZoomRx's most active internal advocate at Pfizer Vaccines. She was responsible for referrals and exploring new offerings.

Mitigation: Build Aimee Renaud (AI Excellence) and Tim Douglas into comparable advocacy roles. Aimee brings above-brand reach; Tim brings established trust and repeat-buyer credibility. Together, they can fill the gap Lisa left.

Relationship Health Assessment

Dimension Rating Evidence
Trust Strong (Vaccines) / Unestablished (Non-Vaccine) 4 HCPP engagements running successfully; repeat business from Tim Douglas and Gabby Gonzalez
Breadth Narrow Single TA (Vaccines), with emerging non-vaccine footholds (Message Labs, Lorbrena)
Depth Moderate Multiple services on Abrysvo Maternal (PET + HCPP + Digital); single-service on other brands
Advocacy Moderate (weakened by Lisa Dixon loss) Tim Douglas is a repeat buyer but not an active evangelist; Aimee Renaud is emerging as above-brand advocate
Strategic Positioning Strong AI pioneer reputation; HCPP methodology differentiation; portfolio-level insights (communication style findings)
Revenue Trajectory Growth 90% growth target FY25 to FY26; if achieved, positions for $700K+ FY27

Key Takeaway

The Pfizer relationship is at a critical juncture: credibility is established, AI reputation is strong, and the HCPP wedge is proven. The next 12 months will determine whether Pfizer stays a mid-tier vaccines-only account ($300-500K) or becomes a diversified, multi-TA engagement ($700K+ in FY27). The difference hinges on three things: (1) converting Message Labs / Aimee Renaud into non-vaccine brand entries, (2) winning at least one PET from BI, and (3) building new internal advocates to replace Lisa Dixon. The relationship's structural defensibility today is moderate — HCPP entrenchment on 4 vaccine brands is meaningful, but the lack of annual contracts and cross-TA breadth means a single budget event could significantly reduce revenue. Every new brand and new service type added is an incremental moat.

Back to Accounts Hub | Home
